summary: Leading French Symbolist poet, known for dense, multilayered poetry and typographical experimentation. Most commonly identified by 'The Afternoon of a Faun' (which inspired Debussy's Prelude), 'A Throw of the Dice Will Never Abolish Chance' (a visually revolutionary poem with varied typefaces and spacing), and his Tomb poems (for Poe, Baudelaire, and Gautier). Hosted the Tuesday Salons (Mardistes) and mentored Paul Valéry.

works.name: The Afternoon of a Faun (L'Après-midi d'un faune)
works.description: Pastoral eclogue in which a half-goat faun lusts after nymphs upon waking from a daytime nap. Inspired Debussy's famous Prelude.

clues.clue: Opens 'These nymphs, I would perpetuate them' — faun speaker discusses meeting nymphs during a morning dream
examples: These nymphs, I would perpetuate them
a mythological creature discusses meeting some nymphs during a morning dream

clues.clue: Inspired Debussy's Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun — opening flute melody C-sharp to G and back
examples: inspired a 'Prelude' by Claude Debussy
An opening melody descending from C-sharp to G natural and back up to C-sharp is played by the flute

clues.clue: Speaker waits/tells nymphs 'I shall see the shade you became' / 'I wait the shade that you become'
examples: ended one poem by declaring 'I shall see the shade you became'
tells two virgin nymphs 'I wait the shade that you become'

clues.clue: Speaker asks the faun/creature 'if the women of your glosses / are the phantoms of your fabulous desires'
examples: asks the title creature 'if the women of your glosses / are the phantoms of your fabulous desires'

clues.clue: Envisions Venus walking on 'lava-flesh' of Mount Etna
examples: envisions Venus walking on the 'lava-flesh' of Mount Etna

clues.clue: Early typography experiments — fully capitalized words like 'MEMORIES' and 'RENDER'
examples: features early instances of its author's experiments with typography, such as the capitalization of words like 'MEMORIES' and 'RENDER'

clues.clue: Ravel wrote three art songs based on his poems; Boulez's Pli selon pli has improvisations on his poems; Hindemith ballet
examples: Maurice Ravel wrote a series of three art songs based on poems by this poet
Pierre Boulez's Pli selon pli includes three improvisations on poems by this author

clues.clue: Herodiade — 34-year poetic retelling of Salome's marriage; 'Aurora dragged her wings in the basin's tears'; 'moribund star, which never more shall shine'
examples: That poetic retelling of the marriage of Salome was written over a period of 34 years
describes an 'Aurora' that 'dragged her wings in the basin's tears'

works.name: A Throw of the Dice Will Never Abolish Chance
works.description: Visually revolutionary 20-page poem with varied typefaces, spacing, and large amounts of blank space. Describes 'eternal circumstances from the depth of a shipwreck.'

clues.clue: 20-page poem with varied typefaces, font sizes, and liberal white space — landmark typographical experimentation
examples: typeset with varying font and spacing
printed across 11 open-face pages in varying font sizes

clues.clue: 'Eternal circumstances / from the depth of a shipwreck' — 'EVEN WHEN THROWN INTO ETERNAL CIRCUMSTANCES'
examples: eternal circumstances / from the depths of a shipwreck
EVEN WHEN TRULY CAST IN THE ETERNAL CIRCUMSTANCE OF A SHIPWRECK'S DEPTH

clues.clue: 'All Thought emits' the title event — 'a throw of the dice'
examples: all thought expresses' the title 'Throw of the Dice'
'All thought emits' the title event

clues.clue: Describes a constellation 'cold with neglect and disuse' on one of the final pages
examples: described a constellation cold with neglect and disuse in one of the final pages

clues.clue: Quentin Meillassoux: the 'unique Number' is 707; also 707 words in the poem — The Number and the Siren
examples: Quentin Meillassoux argues that the 'unique number' in a poem by this author is 707
Quentin Meillassoux determined that this Mallarme poem's 'unique Number, which cannot be another' is 707

clues.clue: Wrote 'NOTHING' in all caps followed by 'of the memorable crisis / or might'
examples: wrote 'NOTHING' in all caps followed by 'of the memorable crisis / or might'

examples: turned into an artist's book by Marcel Broodthaers

works.name: Tomb Poems (Tombeaux)
works.description: Sonnets of homage for dead poets — Poe, Baudelaire, and Gautier.

clues.clue: 'The Tomb of Edgar Allan Poe' — describes Poe as 'the Poet with a naked sword' / 'the Poet rouses with two-edged naked sword'; 'black flights of Blasphemy scattered in the future'
examples: describes how 'the Poet rouses with two-edged naked sword'
claims that their inability to decorate a block of granite sets a limit to 'the black flights of Blasphemy scattered in the future'

clues.clue: 'The Tomb of Charles Baudelaire' — 'the buried temple through the sewer's dark' / 'abominable statue of Anubis, the muzzle lit like a ferocious snout'
examples: wrote of 'the buried temple through the sewer's dark' in a poem about the death of this fellow French poet
described 'some abominable statue of Anubis, the muzzle lit like a ferocious snout' at the tomb of another poet

clues.clue: 'Funeral Toast' (for Théophile Gautier) — calls Gautier the 'emblem fatal' of 'our joyousness'
examples: called one author the 'emblem fatal' of 'our joyousness'

clues.clue: Uses multiples of seven lines in his tomb sonnets; Edouard Manet illustrated his translation of Poe's 'The Raven'
examples: Mallarme used multiples of seven lines in his sonnets memorializing other French poets

works.name: Sonnet in -yx
works.description: Sonnet in which six of its lines end with the sound '-yx'; opens 'pure nails raised to consecrate their onyx.'

clues.clue: Six lines end in the sound '-yx' — opens 'pure nails raised to consecrate their onyx'; uses words like 'ptyx'
examples: wrote of 'pure nails raised to consecrate their onyx' in a sonnet in which six of its lines end in the sound -yx
used words like 'onyx' and 'ptyx' in his 'Sonnet en -yx'

clues.clue: Alludes to the Pleiades as a 'septet of scintillations'

clues.clue: Unfinished project called 'The Book' — spent 30+ years on it; urged 'let us have no more of that back and forth motion of our eyes'
examples: spent more than 30 years working on an unfinished project he called The Book
claimed to 'raise my knife' at 'books that are read in the ordinary way'

examples: left over 200 pages of notes for the tomb of a person of this name, his eight-year-old son who died of rheumatism

clues.clue: 'I say: a flower' and 'there arises, musically' a flower 'absent from every bouquet' — from prose work 'Crisis in Poetry'

clues.clue: 'Azure' poem ends strikingly: 'I am haunted. Azure! Azure! Azure! Azure!'

comprehensive_summary:
Stéphane Mallarmé was a leading French Symbolist poet known for dense, multilayered poetry and typographical experimentation. He hosted the Tuesday Salons (Mardistes) in Paris and mentored Paul Valéry, whose 'Great Silence' was catalyzed by Mallarmé's death.

'The Afternoon of a Faun' (L'Après-midi d'un faune) is his most frequently identified poem. It opens 'These nymphs, I would perpetuate them' and features a half-goat faun speaker who lusts after nymphs upon waking from a daytime nap. The speaker waits to 'see the shade you became' and asks if 'the women of your glosses are the phantoms of your fabulous desires.' It envisions Venus on the 'lava-flesh' of Mount Etna. This poem inspired Debussy's famous Prelude (with its opening flute melody C-sharp to G and back). Ravel wrote three art songs based on Mallarmé's poems, and Boulez's Pli selon pli contains three improvisations on his poems.

'A Throw of the Dice Will Never Abolish Chance' is a visually revolutionary 20-page poem using varied typefaces, font sizes, and liberal white space. It describes 'eternal circumstances / from the depth of a shipwreck' and states 'All Thought emits' the title event. It contains a constellation 'cold with neglect and disuse' near the end. Quentin Meillassoux determined the poem's 'unique Number' is 707 (also the number of words) in The Number and the Siren. Marcel Broodthaers turned it into an artist's book.

The Tomb poems (tombeaux) are sonnets of homage: 'The Tomb of Edgar Allan Poe' describes the 'Poet with a naked sword' and 'black flights of Blasphemy'; 'The Tomb of Charles Baudelaire' describes 'the buried temple through the sewer's dark' and an 'abominable statue of Anubis'; 'Funeral Toast' for Théophile Gautier calls him the 'emblem fatal.' The 'Sonnet in -yx' has six lines ending in '-yx,' opening 'pure nails raised to consecrate their onyx,' and alludes to the Pleiades as a 'septet of scintillations.'

Other works include the unfinished Herodiade (34-year poetic retelling of Salome), the conceptual project The Book (30+ years), and notes for 'A Tomb for Anatole' (his son who died age 8 of rheumatism). 'Sea Breeze' opens 'Sad flesh. And I've read all the books.' 'Azure' ends 'I am haunted. Azure! Azure! Azure! Azure!' His prose work 'Crisis in Poetry' includes 'I say: a flower' and a flower 'absent from every bouquet.'
recursive_suggestions: The Afternoon of a Faun — has music answerlines (Debussy Prelude)
A Throw of the Dice — may have standalone appearances
